1: Come, all who are thirsty — come to the waters; you who have no money, come, buy and eat! Come, buy wine and milk without money and without cost (chinnam). The marketplace-crier form was familiar in ANE cities: vendors advertised goods publicly in the open bazaar. The radical inversion is that the best goods — water, wine, milk — are offered chinnam (the same gratis-word as Isaiah 52:3). Water was precious in the ancient Near East; wine and milk were luxury commodities. The offer of all three without payment overturns the economics of survival and desire simultaneously.
2: Why do you spend money on what is not bread, and your wages on what does not satisfy? The critique targets the Babylonian economy of desire: the exile community spending on food-substitutes (idol-temple feasts, Babylonian religious participation) that provide no genuine nourishment. The metaphor of wages-for-satisfaction is an economic diagnostic — the community is getting a poor return on its spiritual investment. YHWH offers the real commodity (covenant relationship) at no cost while the false goods consume resources.
3: Come, hear, and you will live — and I will make an everlasting covenant with you, the sure mercies I promised to David (hasde david ha-ne'emanim). The Davidic covenant's promised mercies (hesed in the plural = repeated acts of covenant loyalty) are extended beyond the single Davidic king to the entire restored community. This democratization of the Davidic promise is one of Second Isaiah's most significant theological innovations: the covenant benefits that flowed through the royal line are redistributed to the people as a whole.
4: See, I made him a witness to the nations, a leader and commander for the peoples. David's historical role as international figure — the king whose fame spread to surrounding nations — is here repurposed as template for Israel's restored role. The Davidic titles (witness, leader, commander) are conferred on the covenant community in its new-Exodus configuration. Israel becomes the testimony-bearer to the nations in place of the Davidic monarchy.
5: You will summon a nation you did not know, and a nation that did not know you will run to you, because of YHWH your God and the Holy One of Israel, for he has glorified you. The unknown-nation coming to Israel reverses the direction of ANE imperial attraction: normally small nations were drawn to imperial capitals. The ingathering is not because of Israel's inherent attractiveness but because YHWH has glorified her — the divine reputation draws international attention to the restored community.
6: Seek the LORD while he may be found; call on him while he is near. The kairos warning — the appointed time of access is not permanent. This echoes the time of favor (et ratzon) of Isaiah 49:8: there is a window of divine availability that must be seized. The prophetic tradition consistently insisted that the moment of YHWH's turning toward the people was an opportunity, not a permanent default condition. Missing the window is the tragedy of Israel's recurring covenant failure.
7: Let the wicked forsake his way and the unrighteous man his thoughts; let him return (shuv) to YHWH, who will have compassion on him, and to our God, who will abundantly pardon. The teshuvah (shuv = return/repentance) theology of the prophetic tradition is articulated in its fullest positive form: YHWH will abundantly (yarbe) pardon — multiplicative, not minimal forgiveness. The word pardon (lisloach) is the specific term for covenant amnesty; YHWH's pardon is not reluctant or conditional on adequate contrition but lavishly available.
8: For my thoughts are not your thoughts, and your ways are not my ways, declares YHWH. The incomprehensibility of divine perspective is stated as the theological ground for lavish pardon: human moral accounting would not forgive so freely. The verse connects the abundant pardon of Isaiah 55:7 to the infinite qualitative gap between divine and human cognition. This is not an evasion of theodicy but an explanation for why YHWH acts in ways that seem logically improbable from within human honor-shame calculus.
9: For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts. The vertical spatial metaphor for the gap between divine and human cognition uses the most obvious dimension of ANE cosmology: the sky's unreachable height above the land. The heaven-earth axis was the fundamental spatial structure of the ANE worldview, with YHWH located in the highest heaven (shamayim ha-shamayim). His thoughts operate at the level of the highest heaven; human thoughts at the level of dry ground.
10: For as the rain and snow come down from heaven and do not return there until they have watered the earth, making it sprout and bear fruit, giving seed to the farmer and bread to the one who eats. The agricultural water cycle was one of the most carefully observed natural processes in the ancient world: rain fell, watered, produced, fed, and then evaporated back. The cycle was reliable — not random. YHWH's word has this same invariable efficacy: it does not return empty any more than precipitation returns without having watered.
11: So shall my word be that goes out from my mouth — it shall not return to me empty but shall accomplish what I intend and succeed in what I send it to do. The divine word as active agent is a consistent Isaianic theme: the word sent on a mission (cf. Isaiah 9:8 — a word sent against Jacob). Here the word's efficacy is guaranteed by the precipitation analogy. In ANE theology, divine decrees and royal edicts were self-executing — the great king's word created the reality it described. YHWH's word operates at this level but without the failures that occasionally undercut royal decrees.
12: For you will go out with joy and be led forth in peace — the mountains and the hills shall burst into song before you, and all the trees of the field shall clap their hands. The cosmic celebration of the new Exodus mirrors Isaiah 49:13 and the Psalms of creation-praise. Mountains singing and trees clapping are the natural world joining the covenant celebration. The RSV-familiar image of nature's joy is an ANE hymnic convention: creation praises YHWH's victories by personified rejoicing. The joyful, peaceful departure contrasts with the haste-and-fear of the Egyptian Exodus.
13: Instead of the thorn the cypress shall come up, and instead of the brier the myrtle shall come up — a memorial to YHWH, an everlasting sign that will never be cut off. The vegetation reversal (thorn → cypress, brier → myrtle) is the Eden-restoration pattern: the curse-vegetation of Genesis 3:18 gives way to the fragrant, useful trees of the renewed creation. The myrtle (hadas) was a ceremonial plant in Israel (Nehemiah 8:15; Zechariah 1:8-11). The reversal becomes a permanent covenant sign embedded in the landscape.
